title: 第 9 節
en: The real revival, however, of Zoroastrianism as the national religion of Persia and the final constitution of the Avestic canon were due, no doubt, to the Sassanians. We read in the Dinkard that Artakshatar (Ardeshir), the son of Papak, king of kings (A.D. 226-240), summoned Tosar and other priests to the capital to settle the true doctrine of the old religion. His son, Shahpuhar (A.D. 240-271), followed his example, and brought together a number of secular writings also, scattered about, as we are told, in the country, in India, Greece, and elsewhere, and ordered their collocation with the Avesta. After that a correct copy was deposited once more in the treasury of Shapigaén.

Shahpuhar 11 (Sapores), the son of Atharmazd (A.D. 309-379), seems to have done for the Avestic religion very much what Constantine was doing about the same time for Christianity. He convoked a ‘tribunal for the controversy of the inhabitants of all regions, and brought all statements to proper consideration and investigation.’ The heresy with which Shahpuhar II and Attrpad had to deal was probably that of Manichaeism. The doctrines of Mani had been spreading so widely during the third century that even a king, Shahpuhar I, was supposed to have THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 4] embraced them. Thus while Constantine and Athanasius settled the orthodox doctrines of Christianity at Nicaea, 325 A.D., Shahpuhar II and Atarpad, the son of Maraspand, were engaged in Persia in extinguishing the heresy of Mani and restoring Mazdaism to ae original purity. The collecting of he Nasks and the numbering of them as twenty-one, is ascribed to Atarpad. Prof. Darmesteter (Introd. p. xxxix) supposes that at his time it was still possible to make additions to the Avestic texts, and he points out passages in the Vendidad which may have reference to the schism of Mani, if not even to Christianity, as known in the East.

At a still later time, under Khitsréi (Khosroes), called Anésharuvan, the son of Kavad (A.D. 531-579), we read that new heresies had to be suppressed, and that a new command was given for ‘the proper consideration of the Avesta and Zend of the primitive Magian statements.’

Soon after followed the Arab conquest, when we are told that the archives and treasures of the realm were once more devastated. Still the Mohammedan conquerors seem to have been far less barbarous than Alexander and his Greek soldiers, for when, after the lapse of three centuries, a new effort was made to collect the Avestic writings, Attr-farnbagi Farukhozadan was able to make a very complete collection of the ancient Nasks. Nay, even at the end of the ninth century, when another high-priest, Atirpad, the son of Himid, the author, or, at all events, the finisher of the Dinkard, made a final collection of the Avesta and Zend, MSS. of all the Nasks seem to have been forthcoming with very few exceptions, whether in the 42 LECTURE II.

ancient Avestic language or in Pehlevi, so that Attwrpad could give in his Dinkard an almost complete account of the Zoroastrian religion and its sacred literature. According to some authorities it was Attr-farnbagi Farukho-zidin who began the Dinkard, while Atfirpad, the son of Himid, finished it. This would place the work between 820 and 890 A.D. Attrpad, or whoever he was, speaks of the twenty-one Nasks or books of the Avesta, as if he had read them either in the original language or in their Pehlevi translation. The only Nask he failed to obtain was the Vastag Νάβις. and the Pehlevi version of the Nadar Nask. We owe all this information partly to Dr. Haug, partly to Dr. West, who has recovered large portions of the MS. of the Dinkard and translated them in volume xxxvii of the Sacred Books of the East.

Of these twenty-one Nasks which, since the days of Attrpid, the son of Maraspand, constituted the Avestic canon, and which are reckoned to have consisted of 345,700 words in Zend, and of 2,094,200 words of Pehlevi (West, 1. ὁ. p. xlv), three only, the 14th, 19th, and 21st, have been saved complete. We are told in one of the Persian Rivayats (δ. B. #. xxxvii. p. 437), that even at the time when the first attempt was made to collect the sacred literature which had escaped the soldiers of Alexander, portions only of each Nask were forthcoming, and none in its original completeness, except the Vindad, i.e. the Vendidad. If we could trust to this statement, it would prove that the division in the Nasks existed even before the time of Attirpad, the son of Maraspand (325 A.D.), and was possibly of Achaemenian origin.

There are fragments of some other Nasks in exist- THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 43 ence, such as the Vistasp sast6, Haddkhto and Bako, but what the Parsis now consider as their sacred canon, consists, besides the Vendidad, of no more than the Yasna, Vispered, Yashts, &c., which contain the bulk of the two other extant Nasks, the Stdd and Bakan Yashts.

The Vendidid contains religious laws and old legends. The Vispered contains litanies, chiefly for the celebration of the six season-festivals, the so-called Gahanbars. The Yasna also contains litanies, but its most important portion consists of the famous Gathas (stem gathd, nom. sing. gatha), metrical portions, written in a more ancient dialect, probably the oldest nucleus round which all the rest of the Avestic literature gathered. The Gathas are found in the Yasna, XXVii-xxxiv, xliii-xlvi, xlvii-l, li, and 11]. Each of these three collections, the Vendidad, Vispered, and Yasna, if they are copied singly, are generally accompanied by a Pehlevi translation and glosses, the socalled Zend. But if they are all copied together, according to the order in which they are required for liturgical purposes, they are without the Pehlevi translation, and the whole collection is then called the Vendidad Sadah, i.e. the Vendidad pure and simple, 1. 6. without commentary.

The remaining fragments are comprehended under the name of Khorda Avesta or Small Avesta. They consist chiefly of prayers such as the five Gah, the Sirézeh, the three Afringdn, the five Nyayish, the Yashts, lit. acts of worship, hymns addressed to the thirty Izads, of which twenty only have been preserved, and some other fragments, for instance, the Hadhokht Nask (S. B. EL. iv. p. xxx ; xxiii. p. 1).

The Parsis sometimes divide the twenty-one Nasks into three classes: (1) the Gathie, (2) the Hadhamathrie, (3) the Law. The Gathic portion represents the higher spiritual knowledge and spiritual duty, the Law the lower worldly duty, and the Hadha-mathrie what is between the two (Dinkard, VIII. 1.5). In many cases, however, these subjects are mixed.

The Gathas are evidently the oldest fragments of the Avestic religion, when it consisted as yet in a simple belief in Ahuramazda as the Supreme Spirit, and in a denial of the Daévas, most of them known to us as worshipped by the poets of the Veda. If Zarathushtra was the name of the founder or reformer of this ancient religion, these Gathas may be ascribed to him. As their language differs dialectically from that of the Achaemenian inscriptions, and as the Pehlevi interpreters, though conversant with the ordinary Avestic language, found it difficult to interpret these Gathas, we are justified in supposing that the Gathic dialect may have been originally the dialect of Media, for it was from Media that the Magi", or the teachers and preachers of the religion of Ahuramazda, are said to have come. It has been pointed out that certain deities, well known in the Veda, and in later Avestic texts, are absent from the Gathas ; for instance, Mithra and Homa; also Andhita and the title of Ameshaspenta (Haug, 1. 6. p. 259). Many abstract concepts, such as Asha, righteousness, Vohtimandé, good thought, have not yet assumed a definite mythological personality in 1 Magi, the Magavas of the Gathas, the Magush in the cuneiform inscription, the Mog of oe times, Haug, p. 169 n., possibly the rab mag of Jerem. xxxix.
2 Darmesteter, S. B. zB, iv. p. xlvi, gives all the evidence for assigning the origin of Zoroaster’s religion to Media.

THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 45 the chapters composed in the Gathie dialect (Haug, p. 171). And what is more important still, the Angré Mainyu or Ahriman of the later Avestic writings has in the Gathas not yet been invested with the character of the Evil Spirit, the Devil, the constant opponent of Ahuramazda! (Haug, |.c. pp. 303-4) I eall this important, because in the cuneiform inscriptions also this character does not, and we may probably be justified in saying, does not yet occur. The early Greek writers also, such as Herodotos, Theopompos, and Hermippos, though acquainted with the Magian doctrine of a dualism in nature and even in the godhead, do not seem to have known the name of Ahriman. Plato knew the name of Ahuramazda, for he calls Zoroaster the son of Oromasos, which must be meant for Ahuramazda, but he too never mentions the name of Angro Mainyu or Areimanios. Aristotle may have known the name of Areimanios as well as that of Oromasdes, though we have only the authority of Diogenes Laertius (Prooem. ὁ. 8) for it. Later writers, both Greek and Roman, are well acquainted with both names.

I mention all this chiefly in order to show that there are signs of historical growth and historical decay in the various portions of what we call Avestic literature. If with Dr. Haug we place the earliest Gatha literature in about 1000 to 1200 B.c., which of course is a purely hypothetical date, we can say at all events that the Gathas are in thought, if not in language also, older than the inscriptions of Darius; that they belonged to Media, and existed there probably before the time of Cyrus and his conquest of the Persian empire.
When we come to the time of Alexander, we see 46 LECTURE II.

that there existed then so large an amount of sacred literature, that we cannot be far wrong in ascribing the whole of the twenty-one Nasks to a pre-Achaemenian period, before 500 B.c. Here we can distinguish again between the old and the later Yasna. The Vendidad, Vispered, the Yashts, and the smaller prayers may be ascribed to the end of the Avestic period. Dr. Haug places the larger portion of the original Vendidad at about 1000-900 8. σ.. the composition of the later Yasna at about 800-700 Ρ.6.

The Pehlevi literature may have begun soon after Alexander. Linguistic chronology is, no doubt, of a very uncertain character. Still, that there is an historical progress both in language and thought from the Gathas to the Yasna, and from the Yasna to the Yashts, can hardly be doubted. Real historical dates are unfortunately absent, except the mention of Gaotama in the Fravardin Yasht (16). If this is meant for Gautama, the founder of Buddhism, we can hardly be wrong in supposing that this name of Buddha had reached Bactria during the first century after Buddha's death, say 477-377 B.c. In later times the presence of 3uddhists in Bactria cannot be doubted!. About the same time coins had been struck with inscriptions in Pehlevi, which must have been the language of the 1 The presence of Buddhists in Bactria in the first century B.c. is attested by several authorities. Alexander Polyhistor, who wrote between 80-60 B.c. (as quoted by Cyrillus contra Julian.), mentions among philosophers the Samanyioi among the Persian Bactrians, the Magoi among the Persians, and the Gymnosophists among the Indians. These Samanyioi were meant for Buddhists. Later still Clemens of Alexandria, Strom. i. p. 359, speaks of Samanaioi among the Bactrians and of Gymnosophists among the Indians, while Eusebius (Praep. Ev. vii. 10) speaks of thousands of Brahmans among Indians and Bactrians. See Lassen, Ind. Alterthumskunde, 11. Ὁ. 1075 ; Spiegel, γα. Alterthumskunde. 1. 671.

THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 47 people about the time of Alexander's conquests. The Avestie language, however, continued to be understood for a long time after, so that, under the Parthian and the Sassanian dynasties, interpreters could be found, able to translate and explain the ancient sacred texts. Nay, if M. Darmesteter is right, additions in Avestice continued to be made as late as the fourth century A.D., provided that the passages which he has pointed out in the Vendidad refer to the suppression of the heresy of Mani by king Shahptr II.
The Relation between the Avesta and the Old Testament.

I thought it necessary to enter thus fully into the history of the rise and decline of the sacred literature of Persia, because I wanted to show how impossible it is to institute a satisfactory comparison between the Persian and any other religion, unless we are fully aware of the historical growth of its sacred canon. Though much light had been shed on this subject by Dr. Haug, it is but lately that the valuable translation of the Dinkard, contributed by Mr. West to my Sacred Books of the East, has enabled us to form an independent judgment on that subject. The Persian religion has often been the subject of comparison both with the religion of India and with that of the Jews, particularly after their return from the exile. The chief doctrines which the Jews are supposed to have borrowed from the followers of Zoroaster are a belief in the resurrection of the body, a belief in the immortality of the soul, and a belief in future rewards and punishments. It is well known that these doctrines were entirely, or almost entirely, absent from the oldest phase of religion among the Jews, so that their presence 48 LECTURE II.

in some of the Psalms and the Prophets has often been used as an argument in support of the later date now assigned to these compositions. Here there are no chronological difficulties. These doctrines exist, as we shall see, at least in their germinal stage, in the Gathas, while of the more minute details added to these old doctrines in the later portions of the Avesta, or in the still later Pehlevi writings, there is no trace even in post-exilic books of the Old Testament. This point has been well argued by Prof. Cheyne in the Hapository Times, June, July, August, 1891 1, But there is another point on which we can observe an even more striking similarity between the Old Testament and the Avesta, namely, the strong assertion of the oneness of God. Here, however, it seems to me that, if there was any exchange of thought between the followers of Moses and of Zoroaster, it may have been the latter who were influenced. Thesudden change from the henotheism of the Veda to the monotheism of the Avesta has never been accounted for, and I venture to suggest, though not without hesitation, that it may have taken place in Media, in the original home of the Zoroastrian religion. It was in the cities of Media that a large Jewish population was settled, after the king of Assyria had carried away Israel, and put them in Halah and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes (2 Kings xviii. 11). Now, however difficult an exchange of religious ideas may be between people speaking different languages, the fact of their worshipping either one God or many gods could hardly fail to attract attention. If then the 1 On Possible Zoroastrian Influences on the Religion of Israel. See also Spiegel, Evanische Alterthumskunde, vol. i. pp. 446 seq. I am not convinced by Prof. Cheyne’s remarks in the Academy, July, 1893, p. 44.

THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 49 Jews impressed their neighbours with the conviction that there could be but one God, a conviction which in spite of many backslidings, seems never to have ceased altogether to form part of the national faith of Israel, everything else would naturally have followed, exactly as we find it in the Avesta, as compared with the Veda. One of the ancient gods, the Asura Varuna, was taken as the one and supreme God, the God above all gods, under the name of Ahura Mazda; the other Devas, if they claimed to be gods, were renounced, and those only who could be treated as secondary spirits, were allowed to remain, nay, were increased in number by such spirits or angels as Ameretat, Haurvatat, Vohuman6, and all the rest.

Tam far from saying that this can be strictly proved. _ Neither can it be proved that the belief in a resurrection and immortality was necessarily borrowed by the Jews from the Zoroastrians. For, after all, people who deny the immortality of the soul, can also assert it. All I say is that such a supposition being historically possible, would help to explain many things in the Avesta and its development out of Vedic or pre-Vedic elements, that have not yet any satisfactory explanation.

I am that I am, But there is a still more startling coincidence. You may remember that the highest expression of this Supreme Being that was reached in India, was one found in the Vedic hymns, ‘ He who above all gods is the only God.’ I doubt whether Physical Religion ean reach a higher level. We must remember that each individual god had from the first been invested (4) E 50 LECTURE II.

with a character high above any human character. Indra, Soma, Agni, and whatever other Devas there were in the Vedic Pantheon, had been described as the creators of the world, as the guardians of what is good and right, as all-powerful, all-wise, and victorious over all their enemies. What more then could human language and religious devotion achieve than to speak of one Supreme Being, high above all these gods, and alone worthy of the name of God? We saw that in Greece also a similar exalted conception of the true God had at a very early time found expression in a verse of Xenophanes, who in the face of Zeus, and Apollo, and Athene ventured to say, ‘There is but one God, the best among mortals and immortals, neither in form nor in thought like wnto mortals. This again seems to me to mark the highest altitude which human language can reach in its desire to give an adequate description of the one true God. For though the existence of other immortals is admitted, yet He is supposed to hold his own preeminent position among or above them, and even a similarity with anything human, whether in shape or thought, is distinctly denied, thus excluding all those anthropomorphic conceptions from which even in the best of religions the Deity seems unable altogether to divest itself. The Hebrew Psalmist uses the same exalted language about Jehovah. ‘ Among the gods, he says, as if admitting the possibility of other gods, ‘there is none like unto Thee. And again he calls Jehovah, the great King above all gods, using almost the same expressions as the Vedic Rishi and the old Greek philosopher. The conception of the Supreme Being as we find it in the Avesta, is by no means THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS. 51 inferior to that of Jehovah in the Old Testament. Dr. Haug (Essays, p. 302) goes so far as to say that it is perfectly identical. Ahura Mazda is called by Zarathushtra ‘the Creator of the earthly and spiritual life, the Lord of the whole universe, in whose hands are all creatures. He is the light and the source of light; he is the wisdom and intellect. He is in possession of all good things, spiritual and worldly, such as the good mind (vohu-mané), immortality (ameretad), health (haurvatid), the best truth (asha vahishta), devotion and piety (Armaiti), and abundance of earthly goods (khshathra vairya), that is to say, he grants all these gifts to the righteous man, who is upright in thoughts, words, and deeds. As the ruler of the whole universe, he not only rewards the good, but he is a punisher of the wicked at the same time. All that is created, good or evil, fortune or misfortune, is his work. A separate evil spirit of equal power with Ahura Mazda, and always opposed to him, is foreign to the earlier portions of the Avesta, though the existence of such a belief among the Zoroastrians may be gathered from some of the later writings, such as the Vendidad,’

Coincidences such as these are certainly startling, but to a student of comparative theology they only prove the universality of truth; they necessitate by no means the admission of a common historical origin or the borrowing on one side or the other. We ought in fact rejoice that with regard to these fundamental truths the so-called heathen religions are on a perfect level with the Jewish and the Christian religions.
But suppose we found the same name, the same proper name of the Deity, say Jehovah in the Avesta, E2 52 LECTURE ΤΙ.

or Ahura Mazda in the Old Testament, what should we say? We should at once have to admit a borrowing on one side or the other. Now it is true we do not find the name of Ahura Mazda in the Old Testament, but we find something equally surprising. You may remember how we rejoiced when in the midst of many imperfect and more or less anthropomorphic names given to the deity in the Old Testament, we suddenly were met by that sublime and exalted name of Jehovah, ‘I am that I am.’ It seemed so different from the ordinary concepts of deity among the ancient Jews. What then should we say, if we met with exactly the same most abstract appellation of the deity in the Avesta? Yet, in the Avesta also there is among the twenty sacred names of God, the name ‘Ahmi yat ahmi, ‘I am that 1 am. Shall we read in this coincidence also the old lesson that God has revealed Himself to all who feel after Him, if haply they may find Him, or is the coincidence so minute that we have to admit an actual borrowing? And if so, on which side is the borrowing likely to have taken place? In the Avesta this name occurs in the Yashts. In the Old Testament it occurs in Exodus 111. 18. Chronologically therefore the Hebrew text is anterior to the Avestic text. In Exodus we read:

‘And Moses said unto God, Behold, when I come unto the children of Israel, and shall say unto them, The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you; and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them? And God said unto Moses, 1 am that Iam: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, 1 am hath sent me unto you.’

This passage, as I am informed by the best authori- THE TRUE VALUE OF THE SACRED BOOKS, 53 ties, is now unanimously referred to the Elohistic nill, Kittel, &c., all agree on that point. But does it not look like a foreign thought? What we expect as the answer to the question of Moses, is really what follows in ver. 15, ‘And God said [moreover] unto Moses, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, Jehovah, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob hath sent me unto you; this is my name for ever....’ This is what we expect, for it was actually in the name of Jehovah, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, that Moses brought the people out of Egypt; nor is there any trace of Moses having obeyed the divine command and having appealed to ‘I am that I am, as the God who sent him. Nay, there is never again any allusion to such a name in the Old Testament, not even where we might fully expect to meet with it.
zh: 瑣羅亞斯德教作為波斯民族宗教的真正復興，以及阿維斯陀經典正典的最終確立，無疑應歸功於薩珊王朝。據《丹卡德》記載，帕帕克之子阿爾塔克沙特（Ardeshir），萬王之王（公元226-240年），召集托薩及其他祭司來到首都，以確立古老宗教的真正教義。其子沙普爾（公元240-271年）步其後塵，並將散落於各地——如我們所聞，散佈在該國、印度、希臘及其他地方——的許多世俗著作蒐集起來，命令將其與阿維斯陀相互對照整理。此後，一份正確的抄本再次被存放於沙皮蓋恩的國庫中。

沙普爾二世（薩波爾斯），阿塔馬茲德之子（西元309-379年），似乎為阿維斯陀宗教所做的事，與君士坦丁同時期為基督教所做的相差無幾。他召集了「一個法庭來處理所有地區居民的爭論，並將所有陳述付諸適當的考量與調查」。沙普爾二世與阿特帕德必須對付的異端可能是摩尼教。摩尼的教義在第三世紀傳播如此廣泛，以至於甚至一位國王沙普爾一世，都被認為曾信奉過它。因此，當君士坦丁與亞他那修於西元325年在尼西亞確立了基督教的正統教義時，沙普爾二世與馬拉斯班德之子阿塔帕德，也正在波斯從事滅絕摩尼異端並恢復瑣羅亞斯德教原初純正的工作。納斯克文獻的彙集及將其編號為二十一部，被歸功於阿塔帕德。達美斯特特教授（《導言》第三十九頁）認為在他的時代，仍然有可能對阿維斯陀文獻進行增補，他指出《萬德達德》中的某些段落可能涉及摩尼的分裂，如果不是甚至涉及在東方所知的基督教的話。
到了更後來的時代，在希特斯雷（Khitsréi，即霍斯勞）統治下——他被稱為阿涅沙魯萬（Anésharuvan），是卡瓦德之子（西元531-579年）——我們讀到必須壓制新的異端邪說，並頒布了新的命令，要求「適當地考究原始祆教陳述的阿維斯塔與宾德」。

阿拉伯人的征服隨之而來，據說王國的檔案與寶藏再次遭到毀滅。然而，伊斯蘭征服者似乎遠比亞歷山大及其希臘士兵更文明，因為在相隔三個世紀之後，當人們再度努力蒐集阿維斯陀經文時，阿特爾-法爾巴吉‧法魯霍扎丹（Attr-farnbagi Farukhozadan）得以蒐集到一份相當完整的古代納斯克文本。更甚者，直至九世紀末期，當另一位大祭司阿蒂帕德（Atirpad），亦即希米德之子——《頭韻經》（Dinkard）的作者或至少是整理者——對《阿維斯陀》（Avesta）及《宗義經》（Zend）進行最終蒐集時，各納斯克的手稿似乎幾乎完全保存下來，極少有缺失，無論是在第二講第42頁。

古代阿維斯陀語或帕拉維語，使得阿特瓦帕德能在其《丹卡德》中對瑣羅亞斯德教及其聖典文獻作出幾乎完整的陳述。據某些權威的說法，是阿特爾—法恩巴吉‧法魯克霍—齊丁開始編纂《丹卡德》，而希米德之子阿特菲帕德完成了它。這樣的話，該著作的成書時間應在西元八二○年至八九○年之間。阿特帕德，或無論他是誰，在談及阿維斯陀的二十一部《那斯克》或經書時，彷彿他曾以原始語言或帕拉維語譯本的形式閱讀過它們。他唯一未能獲得的是《瓦斯塔格‧那貝斯》和《那達爾那斯克》的帕拉維語版本。我們對所有這些信息的瞭解，一部分要感謝郝格博士，一部分要感謝韋斯特博士，後者已復原了《丹卡德》手稿的大部分內容，並在《東方聖典》第三十七卷中對其進行了翻譯。

這二十一部《那斯克》（Nasks）自阿特帕德（Attrpid）——瑪剌斯潘德之子——的時代以來，構成了《阿維斯陀》的正典，據稱曾包含三十四萬五千七百字的《增德》，和二百零九萬四千二百字的《帕赫來維文》（West, l. c. p. xlv）。其中僅有第十四、十九及二十一部三部完整保存。據一部波斯《日瓦亞特》文獻記載（δ. B. #. xxxvii. p. 437），即便在首次嘗試蒐集逃脫亞歷山大士兵摧毀之厄運的聖典文獻時，各部《那斯克》也都只有片段留存，唯《毘達頻陀》（Vindad, i.e. Vendidad）例外保有其原本的完整面目。倘若此說堪信，它將證明《那斯克》的分卷制度甚至存在於阿特帕德——瑪剌斯潘德之子（西元三百二十五年）的時代之前，且可能源於阿契美尼帝國時期。

現存還有一些其他《那斯克》(Nasks) 的殘篇，如《維斯塔斯帕聖典》(Vistasp sasté)、《哈德赫圖》(Haddkhto) 和《巴科》(Bako)，但祆教徒現今所認可的聖典正經，除了《萬迪達德》(Vendidad) 之外，不過就是《雅斯納》(Yasna)、《維斯佩德》(Vispered)、《雅什特》(Yashts) 等典籍而已，這些典籍包含了現存其他兩部《那斯克》的主體——《斯塔德雅什特》(Stdd Yashts) 和《巴坎雅什特》(Bakan Yashts)。

《破壞魔鬼經》(Vendidad) 包含宗教法律與古老傳說。《六禮經》(Vispered) 包含頌禱文，主要用於慶祝六個季節祭典，即所謂的蓋罕巴爾節 (Gahanbars)。《雅斯那經》(Yasna) 亦包含頌禱文，但其最重要的部分乃是著名的《蓋塔經》(Gathas，詞幹 gathd，單數主格 gatha)，係用更古老的方言寫成的韻文，可能是最古老的核心，所有其餘的阿維斯陀文獻都圍繞其周圍而集結。《蓋塔經》見於《雅斯那經》第二十七至三十四篇、第四十三至四十六篇、第四十七至五十篇、第五十一篇及五十二篇。這三部文獻集成——《破壞魔鬼經》、《六禮經》及《雅斯那經》——若各自單獨抄寫，通常附隨一份帕提亞語 (Pehlevi) 譯文及註解，即所謂的《聖語》(Zend)。但若按照禮儀用途所需的次序將它們一同抄寫，則不附帕提亞語譯文，此時全部文獻集合稱為《純粹破壞魔鬼經》(Vendidad Sadah)，即不附註釋的《破壞魔鬼經》本身。

其餘的片段統稱為《小阿維斯陀》（Khorda Avesta）。它們主要包括各種祈禱文，如五種《蓋汗》（Gah）、《西雷則》（Sirézeh）、三種《阿夫林根》（Afringdn）、五種《尼亞依什》（Nyayish）、《雅什特》（Yashts，字面意為「崇拜行為」），即獻給三十位伊查德（Izads）的讚美詩，其中僅保存了二十位的讚美詩，以及其他一些片段，例如《哈德霍赫特·納斯克》（Hadhokht Nask）（S. B. EL. iv. p. xxx; xxiii. p. 1）。
帕西人（Parsis）有時將二十一部《那斯克》（Nasks）分為三類：（1）《加瑟》（Gathie），（2）《哈達瑪特里》（Hadhamathrie），（3）《法律》（Law）。《加瑟》部分代表較高的精神知識與精神責任，《法律》代表較低的世俗責任，而《哈達瑪特里》則介於兩者之間（《丁卡德》VIII. 1.5）。然而在許多情況下，這些主題往往相互混雜。

《迦陀》（Gathas）顯然是阿維斯陀宗教最古老的殘篇，當時該宗教仍然只是對阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahuramazda）作為最高精神的簡單信仰，以及對提婆的否定——其中大多數提婆為我們所知，乃是吠陀詩人所崇拜的對象。若薩拉圖什特拉（Zarathushtra）是這一古老宗教的創立者或改革者的名字，則這些《迦陀》可以歸屬於他。由於其語言在方言上不同於阿卡美尼德銘文的語言，又由於帕赫來維譯者儘管熟悉普通的阿維斯陀語言，卻發現難以解釋這些《迦陀》，我們有理由推測《迦陀》方言可能原本是米底亞的方言，蓋阿胡拉‧馬茲達宗教的教師與傳教者——所謂「祆教司祭」（Magi）——據說即來自米底亞。已有人指出，在吠陀與後期阿維斯陀文獻中為人所熟知的某些神靈，在《迦陀》中卻付之闕如；例如密特拉（Mithra）與何麻（Homa）；以及安迪塔（Andhita）與不朽聖者（Ameshaspenta）的名號（豪格，頁259）。許多抽象概念，如阿薩（Asha，正義）、沃赫蒂曼德（Vohtimandé，善念），在《迦陀》中尚未呈現出明確的神話人格化特徵¹ ¹ 祆教司祭，迦陀中作Magavas，楔形文字銘文中作Magush，中古時代作Mog，豪格，頁169注），可能與《耶利米書》第三十九章的rab mag相關。
2 達梅斯特特（Darmesteter），《梨俱吠陀聖歌英譯本》（S. B. zB）第四卷，頁四六，提供了將瑣羅亞斯德宗教之起源歸屬於美地亞的全部證據。

蒂亞方言（Gathie dialect）所撰寫的章節（Haug, p. 171）。更重要的是，後期阿維斯陀文獻中的安格雷‧美因尤（Angré Mainyu）或阿赫里曼（Ahriman）在《迦薩經》中尚未被賦予邪靈、魔鬼、阿胡拉‧瑪茲達（Ahuramazda）不斷對手的性格！（Haug, l.c. pp. 303-4）我認為這很重要，因為在楔形文字銘文中也沒有，而且我們或許有理由說，這一角色尚未出現。早期的希臘作家，如赫羅多德（Herodotos）、西奧龐波斯（Theopompos）和赫爾米普斯（Hermippos），雖然熟悉瑣羅亞斯德教關於自然與神性本身之二元論的教義，但似乎都不曾聽聞阿赫里曼的名字。柏拉圖知道阿胡拉‧瑪茲達的名字，因為他稱瑣羅亞斯德為俄羅馬蘇斯（Oromasos）之子，這必然是指阿胡拉‧瑪茲達，但他也從未提及安格羅‧美因尤（Angro Mainyu）或阿雷伊曼紐斯（Areimanios）的名字。亞里斯多德可能知道阿雷伊曼紐斯及俄羅馬斯德斯（Oromasdes）的名字，儘管我們只有迪奧根尼斯‧拉爾提烏斯（Diogenes Laertius）的記載（Prooem. ὸ. 8）作為根據。後來的希臘和羅馬作家都對這兩個名字很熟悉。
我提及以上種種，主要是為了表明在我們所謂的阿維斯陀文獻的各個部分中，都存在著歷史成長和歷史衰落的跡象。如果我們隨同豪格博士將最早的迦陀文獻置於公元前一千至一千二百年左右——當然這純粹是一個假設性的年代——那麼我們無論如何可以說，迦陀在思想上若不是在語言上也是如此，都比大流士的銘文更為古老；它們屬於美地亞，並且很可能在居魯士時代及其征服波斯帝國之前就已經存在於那裡。
當我們來到亞歷山大時代時，我們看到
當時存在如此龐大的宗教文獻，以至於我們將全部二十一部《那斯克》歸屬於前阿契美尼德時期（西元前五百年之前）應該沒有太大偏誤。在此，我們可以再次區分古老的《雅斯納》與較晚期的《雅斯納》。《萬迪達德》、《維斯佩德》、《亞什特》及較小的禱文可歸屬於阿維斯陀語時期的末期。豪格博士將原始《萬迪達德》的大部分置於約西元前一千至九百年，而將較晚期《雅斯納》的成書時間置於約西元前八百至七百年。

佩赫萊維文獻可能在亞歷山大之後不久開始出現。語言年代學無疑具有高度不確定的性質。然而，從迦陀（Gathas）到雅斯納（Yasna），再從雅斯納到雅什特（Yashts），語言和思想都存在歷史進展，這幾乎是無可否認的。不幸的是，除了在《弗拉瓦爾丁雅斯納》（Fravardin Yasht）（16）中提及高婆摩（Gaotama）外，實際的歷史日期付之闕如。倘若此指的是佛教創立者喬達摩，我們幾乎不會有誤認為這個佛陀之名在佛陀涅槃後的第一世紀（約西元前四七七至三七七年）已傳入巴克特里亞的可能性。在後來的時代，巴克特里亞存在佛教徒的情形無可置疑¹。大約同一時期，鑄造了帶有佩赫萊維銘文的錢幣，這必定是當時統治者的語言。 --- ¹ 佛教徒在西元前第一世紀在巴克特里亞的存在得到多位權威人士的證實。曾於西元前八十至六十年左右著述的亞歷山大‧波利歷史家（引自基律祿駁異教徒朱利安）提及哲學家中有波斯巴克特里亞人中的薩曼耶人（Samanyioi）、波斯人中的瑪古人（Magoi）及印度人中的裸體修士（Gymnosophists）。這些薩曼耶人本指佛教徒。更晚近的亞歷山大‧克萊門斯《駁異教徒》第一卷第三五九頁提及巴克特里亞人中的薩瑪奈人（Samanaioi）和印度人中的裸體修士，而歐西比烏《福音準備》第七卷第十節則提及印度人和巴克特里亞人中有數千名婆羅門。參見拉森《印度古代學》第二卷第一〇七五頁；施皮格爾《波斯古代學》第一卷第六七一頁。
聖書的真正價值。47 在亞歷山大征服時期前後的人民。然而，阿維斯陀語言在其後很長時間內仍被理解，以至於在帕提亞及薩珊王朝時期，可以找到口譯者，他們能夠翻譯並解釋古代的聖典。更甚者，若達美斯特得（M. Darmesteter）的說法正確，阿維斯陀語的增補一直延續到西元四世紀，但前提是他在《萬迪達》（Vendidad）中所指出的段落確實涉及沙普爾二世國王對馬尼異端的壓制。
阿維斯陀與舊約聖經之間的關係

我認為有必要充分探討波斯聖文獻興衰的歷史，因為我希望示明，除非我們充分認識其聖典的歷史發展，否則不可能在波斯宗教與任何其他宗教之間進行令人滿意的比較。雖然毛格博士（Dr. Haug）已在此問題上闡發了大量見解，但直到最近，韋斯特先生（Mr. West）對《敦克經》（Dinkard）所作的寶貴譯本被收入我的《東方聖書集》（Sacred Books of the East），我們才得以對此問題形成獨立的判斷。波斯宗教長期以來一直被用來與印度宗教以及猶太人宗教進行比較，特別是在他們從流亡中歸返之後。猶太人據說從瑣羅亞斯德（Zoroaster）的追隨者那裡借鑑的主要教義，乃是相信肉體的復活、相信靈魂的不朽性，以及相信未來的獎賞與懲罰。眾所周知，這些教義在猶太人最古老的宗教時期中完全不存在，或幾乎完全不存在，因此它們的出現

在某些《詩篇》和《先知書》中所發現的觀念，常被用來支持現今學者對這些著作所主張的較晚成書年代。但在這方面並無年代學上的困難。如我們將會看到的，這些教義至少在其萌芽階段已存在於《迦薩經》（Gathas）中，而後來《阿維斯塔》（Avesta）的較後部分，乃至更晚的《帕拉維文獻》（Pehlevi writings）中所添加的更細微的細節，即使在《舊約》的流亡後時期著作中也無蹤跡可尋。此點已由切尼教授在《解釋家時報》（Hapository Times）一八九一年六、七、八月刊中論述甚詳¹。但還有另一點，我們可以觀察到《舊約》與《阿維斯塔》之間更為顯著的相似性，即對於上帝唯一性的強烈主張。然而在這方面，在我看來，倘若摩西追隨者與瑣羅亞斯德追隨者之間確有過思想交流的話，受影響者可能是後者。從《吠陀》的單一神教（henotheism）到《阿維斯塔》的一神教（monotheism）的突然轉變，迄今尚未有適當的解釋，而我雖不無猶豫，但仍敢大膽提出：這一轉變可能發生在美地亞，即瑣羅亞斯德宗教的原始故鄉。當亞述王將以色列民族遷移，將他們安置在哈臘、哈博及歌散河邊，以及美地亞城邑中時（《列王紀下》十八章11節），正是在美地亞的城市中，有大量猶太人口得以定居。然而，儘管不同語言的人民之間進行宗教觀念交流是困難的，但他們究竟是祭祀唯一的上帝抑或眾多的神祇這一事實，幾乎不可能不引起注意。如果 ¹ 論可能的瑣羅亞斯德宗教對以色列宗教的影響。又見施皮格爾著《伊朗古代文明史》第一卷第四四六頁以下。我不為切尼教授在《學苑》（Academy）一八九三年七月第四四頁所發表的評論所說服。

猶太人使其鄰邦確信只有唯一的真神存在，這一信念儘管時有衰退，卻似乎從未完全中斷過以色列民族信仰的一部分。倘若事實確然如此，自然而然地，一切都應隨之而來，恰如我們在《阿維斯陀》與《吠陀》的對比中所發現的情況。古代的諸神之一、提婆之王婆樓那（Asura Varuna）被尊為唯一至高的真神——凌駕於一切神祇之上的神——並以阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahura Mazda）之名而稱；其他的提婆若宣稱自身為神，則被摒棄，而只有那些可被視為次級精靈的神祇才得以保留，甚至透過不朽者（Ameretat）、完美者（Haurvatat）、好思者（Vohuman）等此類精靈或天使的增加而數目有所增加。
我遠非聲稱這可以被嚴格地證明。同樣地，猶太人對於復活與不朽的信仰必然是從瑣羅亞斯德教徒借用而來的，這點也無法被證明。因為畢竟，否定靈魂不朽之人，也可以主張它的不朽。我所說的僅是，假設這樣的推測在歷史上是可能的，就足以幫助我們解釋《阿維斯陀經》中的許多事物，以及它如何從吠陀或前吠陀要素發展而來──這些至今還沒有任何令人滿意的解釋。
我就是我所是（I am that I am），但還有更加令人驚異的巧合。你可能記得在印度所達到的關於這位至高存有的最高表達，乃是在吠陀讚歌中所發現的：「超越一切諸神之上，唯一的神。」我懷疑物質宗教是否能達到更高的境界。我們必須記住，每一位獨立的提婆從一開始就被賦予了(4) E 50 講座二。

具有遠超任何人類性質的品格。因陀羅、蘇摩、阿耆尼，以及吠陀萬神殿中的其他一切提婆，都曾被描述為世界的創造者、善與正義的守護者、全能者、全智者，以及戰勝一切敵人的勝利者。那麼，人類語言和宗教虔誠還能比談論一位至高無上的存在者、高於這一切諸神、唯一堪當上帝之名者，而達成更多的事呢？我們看到，在希臘，一個類似的關於真正上帝的崇高觀念也在很早的時期就在克桑方尼的一首詩句中找到了表達。在宙斯、阿波羅和雅典娜面前，他大膽宣稱：「只有一位上帝，是凡人和不朽者中最好的，既不在形體上，也不在思想上像凡人。」這似乎再次標誌出人類語言為了給出對唯一真正上帝之適當描述所能達到的最高境界。因為雖然其他不朽者的存在被承認了，但他被認為在他們之中或之上保持著他自己的卓越地位，而且即使與任何人類事物的相似性，無論是在形體上還是思想上，都被明確否認了，由此排除了那些擬人化的觀念——即使是在最好的宗教中，神聖者似乎也無法完全將其脫去。希伯來詩篇作者對耶和華也使用了同樣崇高的語言。他說「在眾神中」——彷彿承認其他眾神的可能性——「沒有像你一樣的。」他又稱耶和華為高於一切眾神的偉大君王，所用的表述幾乎與吠陀聖人和古代希臘哲學家相同。我們在《阿維斯陀》中所找到的至高存在者的觀念，絲毫不劣於舊約中的耶和華的觀念。豪格博士（《論文集》第302頁）甚至說它是完全相同的。阿胡拉‧馬茲達被瑣羅亞斯德稱為「塵世生命和精神生命的創造者，整個宇宙的主宰，一切生物都掌握在他的手中。他是光和光的源頭；他是智慧和理智。他擁有一切好的事物，無論是精神的還是世俗的，例如良善的思想（vohu-mané）、不朽（ameretad）、健康（haurvatid）、最好的真理（asha vahishta）、虔誠和敬畏（Armaiti），以及塵世財富的豐富（khshathra vairya），也就是說，他將這一切恩賜都賦予那些在思想、言語和行為上正直的義人。作為整個宇宙的統治者，他不僅獎賞善良的人，同時也是邪惡之人的懲罰者。一切被創造的事物，無論是好是壞、幸運是不幸，都是他的作為。一個與阿胡拉‧馬茲達力量相等且始終與他相對的獨立邪惡精靈，對於《阿維斯陀》的早期部分來說是陌生的，雖然瑣羅亞斯德教徒中存在這種信仰的情況可以從《萬迪達德》等一些較晚的著作中看出，
這樣的巧合確實令人驚愕，但對於比較神學的研究者而言，它們只是證明了真理的普遍性；這些巧合絕不必然要求我們承認存在共同的歷史起源，或承認某一方對另一方有所借用。事實上，就這些根本性的真理而言，所謂異教宗教與猶太教及基督教宗教處於完全平等的地位，我們應當為此而欣慰。
但假如我們在《阿維斯陀》中發現了同樣的名字，也就是同樣的神聖專有名，比如說耶和華（Jehovah），

或者在《舊約聖經》中找到阿胡拉‧瑪茲達（Ahura Mazda），我們該作何言論？我們立刻必須承認一方或另一方的借用。誠然，我們在《舊約聖經》中找不到阿胡拉‧瑪茲達的名字，但我們發現了同樣令人驚異的東西。你們可能還記得，當我們在《舊約聖經》中眾多不完美且多少帶有人形特徵的神聖名號之中，突然遭遇那尊崇而崇高的耶和華名號「我是自有永有的」時，我們是何等歡喜。它似乎與古代猶太人對於神聖性的普通概念迥然相異。那麼，倘若我們在《阿維斯陀》（Avesta）中遭遇完全相同的最抽象的神聖名號，我們該作何言論？然而，在《阿維斯陀》中，在神的二十個聖名之中，也有「我是自有永有」（Ahmi yat ahmi）這個名號。我們應當在此巧合中閱讀出那古老的教訓，即上帝向所有尋求祂的人啟示自己，倘或他們尋得祂，或是這個巧合如此微細，以至於我們必須承認確實存在借用？而且若然，借用最有可能是發生在哪一方？在《阿維斯陀》中，此名號出現於《雅什特》（Yashts）。在《舊約聖經》中，它出現於《出埃及記》三章十八節。因此在年代學上，希伯來文本先於阿維斯陀文本。在《出埃及記》中我們讀到：
「摩西對上帝說，看哪，我到以色列人那裡去，對他們說，你們列祖的上帝打發我到你們這裡來。他們必問我說，他叫甚麼名字？我要向他們說甚麼呢？上帝對摩西說，我是自有永有的。又說，你要對以色列人這樣說，那自有的打發我到你們這裡來。」

本段落據最權威的學者告知，現已獲得一致共識，認定出自「以羅興」文獻（Elohistic）——尼爾、基特爾等人在此點上無異議。然而，這豈非看似一種外來的思想？我們期待摩西之問的答案，實際上是隨後第15節所述：「神對摩西說，你要這樣對以色列人說，耶和華你們祖宗的神，亞伯拉罕的神，以撒的神，雅各的神打發我到你們這裡來；這是我的名，直到永遠……」這正是我們所期待的，因為摩西確實是以耶和華、亞伯拉罕、以撒與雅各的神之名義，將人民領出埃及；且毫無跡象表明摩西曾遵從神聖的命令，而去訴諸於「我是自有永有的」（*I am that I am*）作為差遣他的神。反之，在整部舊約聖經中，再也沒有任何關於如此名號的隱喻，即便在我們充分期待遇見它的地方亦然。
